q: How do antibiotics eliminate bacterial infections without harming eukaryotic host cells?
options: Antibiotics disrupt the human cell membrane, releasing bacteria trapped inside
Antibiotics target structures unique to bacteria (70S ribosomes, peptidoglycan cell walls, unique metabolic enzymes) absent in eukaryotic cells
Antibiotics stimulate the human immune system to produce more antibodies
Antibiotics lower body temperature so bacteria cannot replicate at cooler conditions
explanation: Bacterial cells have structural and molecular features not found in eukaryotes (e.g., 70S ribosomes, peptidoglycan walls); antibiotics exploit these differences to kill bacteria selectively.
